a  Modular design, which decomposes the autonomous driving
system into perception, motion, decision-making, planning,
and control modules, has been the mainstream architecture for
autonomous driving due to its design flexibility[1], [2], [3].
In perception task, the evolution of various tasks, including
3D object detection, Bird’s-Eye-View (BEV) detection, and
3D semantic occupancy prediction, has driven vision-centric
autonomous driving towards more advanced stages. Recently,
end-to-end autonomous driving that directly generate planning
results from raw sensors have been extensively researched for
their higher adaptability and robustness in complex scenarios.
Currently, most end-to-end autonomous driving models
follow a pipeline that encompasses perception, motion predic-

tion, and planning. Unlike traditional modular designs, these
models unify different tasks within a single model. Traditional
3D perception methods with explicit supervision are still
employed for scene representation in end-to-end autonomous
driving. For instance, UniAD[4] used dense prediction tasks,
such as map segmentation, object detection, and occupancy
prediction as intermediate explicit outputs to refine features for
planning task. VAD[5], [6] and GenAD[7] adopt vectorized
scene representations, encoding maps and objects as vectors
to improve inference efficiency. Existing end-to-end solutions
continue to follow the mind of traditional perception tasks,
emphasizing the effective perception and understanding of
global information within the field of view.
However, unlike modular architectures that depend on man-
ually designed rules, end-to-end architectures must adaptively
focus on key regions influencing vehicle navigation while
disregarding irrelevant ones, in accordance with a planning-
centric design. For instance, during a lane change, attention
should be directed primarily to vehicles ahead and those
in adjacent lanes behind, while distant, unrelated vehicles

A. Scene Representation in End-to-End Autonomous Driving
Most prior end-to-end autonomous driving approaches
sample explicit scene representation, which derived from
traditional perception tasks, from dense BEV features based
on Transformer framework. ST-P3 [9] introduced the first
end-to-end autonomous driving framework with explicit scene
representation, which consists of object detection and BEV
semantic segmentation, based on surround-view cameras.
UniAD [4], building on prior work,  integrated perception,
tracking, prediction, and planning into a Transformer-based
end-to-end framework, achieving state-of-the-art performance
across all relevant tasks on the nuScenes[8]. In addition to
traditional 3D object detection and tracking, it introduces se-
mantic occupancy prediction for explicit scene representation.
VAD [5], VADv2 [6], and GenAD [7] vectorized the dense
scene representations, simplifying perception tasks through
vectorized BEV object detection and map segmentation,
achieving better performance compared with UniAD[4].
PARA-Drive [10] reorganized the cascaded framework into
a parallel  one,  utilizing semantic maps and occupancy
prediction for scene representation, while deactivating certain

tasks during inference. OccWorld [11] explored a dynamically
evolving scene representation by learning a world model in
3D occupancy space, while predicting both the motion of
the ego-vehicle and the evolution of the surrounding scene.
These methods, which sample from dense BEV features as
the primary scene, increase model complexity, especially for
the dense occupancy prediction tasks[12].
With the emergence of sparse scene representations, recent
sparse end-to-end autonomous driving methods directly inter-
act with task-specific queries and image features. SparseDrive
[13] and SparseAD [14] decouple instance features and
geometric anchors from image features to fully represent
instances,  including dynamic road agents and static map
elements. LAW [15] proposed an innovative self-supervised
approach that predicts future features using a latent world
model and supervises with actual observed features, thereby
enhancing end-to-end driving performance without the need
for  expensive  labels.  It  is worth noting  that  the scene
representations in end-to-end autonomous driving evolve from
dense to sparse, and from sparse to implicit. However, the
emphasis remains on global scene representation,  lacking
explicit guidance and attention to key instance tokens that
impact driving, and lacking interpretability.
B.  Interpretability  for Scene  in End-to-End Autonomous
Driving
With the emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs)
and Vision-Language Models  (VLMs),  research  in au-
tonomous driving has gradually shifted toward more effi-
cient and interpretable architectures and started to integrate
these models  to enhance reasoning and decision-making
capabilities[16]. In Hint-AD [17], a global alignment approach
for interpretability is proposed to improve the transparency
and traceability of decision-making in end-to-end models.
DriveGPT4 [18] explore how to integrate large language
models  into end-to-end autonomous driving systems  to
enhance interpretability and reasoning capabilities.
In addition, vision-language models are also widely applied
in end-to-end autonomous driving. HE-Drive [19] explores
the integration of vision-language models into end-to-end
autonomous driving systems,  enabling more human-like
reasoning and the ability to handle complex driving scenarios.
Similarly, DriveVLM[20] further argue the integration of
vision-language models with autonomous driving technology
that combines visual and language information can enhance
the capabilities of perception and decision-making. Addi-
tionally, DriveLM [21] introduces the integration of visual
question answering with autonomous driving. By employing a
graphical visual question answering model, the system offers
a more flexible and interactive reasoning approach, enabling
driving decisions to be made not only based on raw inputs
but also through a question-and-answer process that further
reasons and validates scene information.
Further,  researchers are increasingly pursuing more in-
tegrated and generalizable models  that not only handle
driving tasks themselves but also address more complex
perception, reasoning, and planning tasks. In Reason2Drive

Overview This section presents our proposed end-to-end
autonomous driving framework with language-guided scene
representation, as illustrated in Fig2. We first  introduce a
universal vectorized scene representation method that captures
high-order map-ego-agent interactions to achieve a global
vectorized scene representationc (Sec 3.1). Next, we then
elaborate on how scene representation attention is guided
by region-focused information generated by large vision-
language models Sec 3.2). Building upon this learned scene
representation, we model scene topology relationships by
using self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms (Sec 3.3)
for comprehensive scene understanding.

A. Universal Vectorized Scene Representation
The goal of end-to-end autonomous driving is to directly
predict future trajectories from raw sensor data. To guide the
prediction of future trajectories, scene representations and
understanding play a crucial role in end-to-end autonomous
driving. Universal scene representation methods build upon
techniques from perception tasks, first extracting Bird’s Eye
View (BEV) features and then querying the BEV features to
extract information, followed by supervised decoding of scene
representations, such as detection[24], [25] and mapping[26].
Image  to BEV.  In end-to-end autonomous driving,  the
conversion from image to bird’s eye view (BEV) features
is a crucial  step in the pipeline. Given the raw image

using a convolutional neural network (CNN)[27]. Then, a
mapping function T is used to project the image features
into the BEV perspective to obtain the BEV feature map

This process involves geometric transformations and ad-
justments to spatial resolution, mapping features from the
camera perspective to the bird’s eye view for subsequent
tasks.

B. Language Guided Insight Scene Representation
Traditional BEV-based perception methods emphasize
global scene representation, which is not always necessary for
end-to-end tasks. Instead, greater attention should be allocated
to regions  that directly influence driving. Therefore, we
propose an insight scene representation that assigns varying
levels of attention to the surroundings based on language
description generated by VLMs.
BEV-Language Alignment. Our goal is to leverage driving-
related language information generated by VLMs to guide
the attention of scene representation. However,  there is a
disparity between the feature spaces of natural language and
scene representations. In response, we propose an Instance-
Language Alignment (ILA) module that aligns the feature
spaces through contrastive learning. This approach effectively
directs  the model  to focus on objects and regions  that
influence driving, thereby enhancing the scene understanding
capabilities of the end-to-end autonomous driving.
The objective is to align the scene representation features
with driving-related language description in the same feature
Rdagent
space. First, we extract scene representations w∈  from
the BEV features  fbev.

These features are used to capture
dynamic and static information by decoding with supervision.
Simultaneously, given the driving-related description Tlanguage,

To align the scene representation features with the language
features, we employ cosine similarity as the similarity metric
and compute the similarity[28]. The similarity between the

y = 1 indicates that the agent-language pair is a valid match,
and y = 0 indicates a non-matching pair.

Through contrastive learning, the model effectively aligns
the agent query features with the driving-related language
information. This alignment mechanism directs scene under-
standing toward the objects and regions critical to the driving
task, significantly enhancing the reasoning capabilities and
accuracy of the end-to-end autonomous driving system.
Insight Scene Description Generation. In our work, we
employ an Image-grounded Text Generation (ITG) method,
which use the Q-Former architecture[29] to transform image
features into driving-related textual descriptions. The input is

The textual representation is obtained using a pre-trained
language model[31]. To bridge the modality gap between
agent-centric features and textual  representations, Query
Transformer  (Q-Former)  is used to achieve cross-modal
alignment to obtain the fused feature. The model is trained
to minimize the negative log-likelihood of the correct token
sequence using cross-entropy loss:

where P(ti | t<i,A) represents the probability of predicting
token ti based on previous tokens and the agent-centric feature
representation. The objective is to maximize the likelihood
of generating accurate and contextually relevant descriptions.
Through this Image-grounded Text Generation method, we
effectively convert visual information into natural language
descriptions related to driving tasks, providing deep scene
understanding for end-to-end autonomous driving systems.
This approach not only improves  the accuracy of scene
representation but  also enhances  the system’s  reasoning
capability in complex driving scenarios.
C. Topology Graph of Scene Representation
In order to model the topology relationships between the
ego vehicle and other agents, we first concatenate the ego
query and agent queries to obtain a unified query vector
Qconcat = [Qego,Qagent], and then apply self-attention over the
concatenated query vector to bulid the topology relationships.

A. Datasets and Evaluation Metrics
Datasets To evaluate the performance of the proposed
InsightDrive framework in autonomous driving tasks, we
conducted extensive experiments on the widely used nuScenes
dataset[8]. The nuScenes dataset consists of 1000 driving
scenes, each providing 20 seconds of RGB video data. The
ego vehicle is equipped with 6 surrounding cameras, each with
a 360 degree horizontal field of view (FOV), as well as a 32-
beam LiDAR sensor. For each keyframe, the nuScenes dataset
provides semantic maps and 3D object detection annotations
at a frequency of 2Hz. We followed the official instructions[8]
to partition the dataset into 700 scenes for training, 150 scenes
for validation, and 150 scenes for testing.
Evaluation Metrics Following existing end-to-end au-
tonomous driving methods VAD[5], we use the L2 displace-
ment error and collision rate to evaluate the performance of the
planned trajectory. The L2 displacement error measures the L2
distance between the planned trajectory and the ground-truth

trajectory, while the collision rate quantifies the frequency of
collisions between the vehicle and other traffic participants
during the execution of the planned trajectory.
B.  Implementation Details.
The experiments use ResNet50[27] as the backbone net-
work to extract image features with the official pre-trained
weights. In the first phase,  the vision-language alignment
module is pre-trained on the NuScenes dataset, while in the
second phase,  the end-to-end model  is trained using pre-
trained weights. The training is carried out on 8 NVIDIA
L20 GPUs using the AdamW optimizer and a cosine learning
−4
rate scheduler. The initial learning rate is set to 2×10  with
a weight decay of 0.01. The model is trained for 60 epochs
with a batch size of 16.
C. Comparisons with State-of-the-art Methods
Our model builds upon the VAD-tiny[5] model  as a
baseline, which is enhanced and optimized to validate the
effectiveness of scene representation guided by language
attention for end-to-end autonomous driving. We compared
InsightDrive with state-of-the-art end-to-end autonomous
driving methods. As shown in TableI, InsightDrive achieves
the best L2 error among all methods. In terms of collision
rate, InsightDrive performs comparably to UniAD[4], which
additionally uses occupancy prediction supervision, but with
a faster inference speed (16.3 FPS vs. 1.8 FPS). Compared
to the VAD-tiny[5] baseline, InsightDrive achieves a 43%
reduction in L2 error and a 60% decrease in collision rate.
D. Ablation Study
Effect of the insight scene representation. We analyzed the
proposed Topology Graph Module(TGM), which constructs
the topology relationships for ego-agents interactions through
self-attention and (ego,agents)-map interactions via cross-
attention. As shown in TableIII,  the TGM significantly
enhances scene understanding and effectively improves the
performance of planning. In addition, language-guided atten-
tion module(LGAM) guides ego vehicle pay more attention
to the elements that influence driving, leading to a significant
performance improvement in the collision rate metric, with a
reduction from 0.37 to 0.15.
Perception and prediction performance. In addition, we
explore the necessity of language-guided attention module.
As shown in TableII, although the model without language-
guided attention achieved superior performance in perception
tasks, its effectiveness in planning tasks was notably lower
compared the language guided approach. Furthermore, we
conducted the experiments on a more comprehensive language
descriptions to determine whether planning tasks benefit more
from a detailed scene representation or attention-based scene
representation. A more comprehensive language descriptions,
which are produced by VLMs, is constrained by the large
model’s ability to perception distant objects and the issue of
occlusion. It is still an attention-based description compared
to perception supervision. However, compared to the attention-
based description we employ, it provides relatively more infor-
mation. We observed a decline in experimental performance

aspects,  including map segmentation,  detection, motion
prediction, planning results, and attention description, along
with surround-view camera inputs for reference comparison.
